topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The patch of earth where the oak once stood was now just a muddy scar, a vacant space where a monument to my juvenile optimism used to reside. I walked over it, the damp soil clinging to my worn boots, and let out a short, sharp exhale that held no warmth. It had been thirty years since I’d watered that sapling, a foolish, hopeful act of childhood idealism. Now, only stumps remained, jagged remnants of what was once something substantial. I traced the rough bark of the remaining roots with a gloved finger, a gesture devoid of reverence. It was a testament to impermanence, a reminder that even the most earnest efforts eventually yield to the blunt force of adult indifference. I turned away quickly, the sight of the cleared ground grating against my sensibilities.

[story 2]

My gaze settled on the exposed roots, tangled and brittle, the remnants of a life I had once nurtured with such careless enthusiasm. A tightness constricted my chest, a familiar, unpleasant clenching in my throat. I stood there for a long moment, arms crossed tightly over my chest, observing the barren field where the tree had stood. There was no sorrow in the observation, only a cool, detached assessment of the poor execution. Someone had come here, with tools and an air of finality, and had erased the evidence of my youthful folly. I shifted my weight, the sound of my leather shoes crunching the dry debris sounding unnecessarily loud in the quiet afternoon. I simply shook my head once, a small, dismissive movement, and continued my path, unwilling to linger in the shadow of such a cheap erasure.

[story 3]

I knelt down, not to mourn, but to examine the cut surface of the trunk. The wood was freshly exposed, a pale, raw wound against the dark earth. A flicker of something sharp and unpleasant sparked behind my eyes. It was the stark reality of things ending—not with a gentle fade, but with a brutal severing. I ran a hand over the splintered edges, feeling the rough texture under my fingertips, a sensation that registered only as irritation. How utterly wasteful. To invest so much hope, so much time, only to have it reduced to mere lumber. I stood up slowly, brushing nonexistent dust from my trousers, my jaw set tight. There was nothing left to say, only a profound sense of being unimpressed by the whole sordid affair.
